title: Autonomic Dysreflexia: A Spinal Cord Injury Emergency
subtitle: A 38-year-old man with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly develops a pounding headache and a blood pressure of 210/110 — far above his usual low baseline. The nurse must recognize autonomic dysreflexia, sit him up, and find and remove the trigger fast.

patient.name: Andre Coleman
patient.admitDate: Rehab unit, T4 spinal cord injury 8 months ago (paraplegia). Sudden severe pounding headache with BP 210/110 (usual baseline ~96/60), flushing and sweating above the injury, and a distended bladder with a kinked indwelling catheter. Treated for autonomic dysreflexia.

vitals.temp: Pale, cool skin with goosebumps below the level

nursesNote: Andre Coleman, 38, T4 spinal cord injury, with abrupt autonomic dysreflexia: pounding headache, BP 210/110 (baseline ~96/60), reflex bradycardia, flushing/sweating above the lesion and pale/cool skin below, triggered by a distended bladder with a kinked catheter. AD occurs with injuries at/above T6 from a noxious stimulus below the level. Priorities: SIT UPRIGHT, loosen constrictive items, find and remove the trigger (bladder first, then bowel with anesthetic lubricant), monitor BP every 2-5 min, give a rapid antihypertensive if it stays high, and reinforce prevention.
providerOrders: Sit the patient upright; lower the legs
Identify and remove the noxious trigger (bladder first, then bowel)
Drain/unkink the urinary catheter; check for impaction using anesthetic lubricant
Monitor BP every 2-5 minutes; give ordered rapid-acting antihypertensive if BP stays high
Reinforce the bladder/bowel program and trigger prevention

opts.text: It occurs in spinal cord injury at or above T6
A sudden, severe pounding headache with dangerously high BP is the hallmark
BP can rise far above the patient's usual low baseline — a true emergency
Above the injury: flushing/sweating/congestion; below: pale, cool skin with goosebumps
A full bladder is the most common trigger
These symptoms are minor and can be watched without acting

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What is driving it and why act fast?

opts.text: It is triggered by a noxious stimulus BELOW the level of injury
The most common triggers are a distended bladder and fecal impaction
Reflex bradycardia often accompanies the severe hypertension
Sitting the patient upright helps lower the blood pressure quickly
Lying the patient flat is the best first action
Untreated, it can cause seizure, stroke, or death

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the management of the episode

opts.text: Sit the patient upright and lower the legs
Loosen any tight clothing or constrictive devices
Check and relieve the bladder first (unkink/drain the catheter)
If not the bladder, check for fecal impaction with anesthetic lubricant
Monitor BP frequently; give antihypertensive if high; document
Sit the patient upright / raise the head of the bed
Check and unkink or drain the urinary catheter first
Loosen tight clothing, leg straps, or abdominal binders
Monitor BP every 2-5 minutes during the episode
Use anesthetic lubricant before checking for impaction
Give the ordered antihypertensive if BP stays dangerously high
Lay the patient flat and elevate the legs
Ignore the headache and recheck BP in an hour
Disimpact the rectum without anesthetic lubricant

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Sit the patient upright immediately to drop the BP
Quickly survey for the trigger while loosening constrictive items
Drain/unkink the catheter or relieve the distended bladder
If unresolved, check for impaction with anesthetic lubricant
Reassess BP, give ordered meds if needed, and reinforce prevention
BP falls and the headache resolves after the bladder is drained
Flushing and sweating subside and the heart rate returns to baseline
BP settles back to the patient's usual lower baseline
The patient and family can name triggers and a bladder/bowel plan
BP keeps climbing with worsening headache and blurred vision
The patient has a seizure from uncontrolled hypertension
The catheter stays kinked and the bladder remains distended
The patient was laid flat and the BP rose further

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Physiological Adaptation, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
